title: The Noise Schedule, Step by Step
concept: At each timestep, a predetermined amount of Gaussian noise is mixed in, following a fixed schedule that controls how fast the image degrades.

exercises.prompt: If you add a tiny drop of food coloring to water every few seconds, what happens over time?

options.text: The water slowly changes color until it's fully colored
The water instantly turns solid color

exercises.explain: Each drop adds a little bit more change. Add enough drops over enough time, and the water goes from clear to fully colored — that's exactly how noise builds up step by step!

cards.title: Not All at Once!
cards.body: Ever wonder how an AI turns a clear photo into pure static? It doesn't do it in one blast — it follows a careful, step-by-step plan called a noise schedule, adding just a little bit of noise at a time.

cards.title: What Is a Noise Schedule?
cards.body: A noise schedule is a fixed plan that tells the model exactly how much random (Gaussian) noise to add at every single timestep. It's decided in advance — like a recipe — so the image degrades in a smooth, predictable, controlled way instead of randomly all at once.

cards.title: Steeping Tea
cards.body: Think of dropping a tea bag into hot water. The color doesn't appear instantly — it spreads out gradually, a little more each second. A noise schedule works the same way: a set amount of 'noise' seeps into the image at every timestep until it's completely clouded.

cards.title: From Cat to Static
cards.body: Step 1: A clear photo of a cat gets a tiny sprinkle of noise — still clearly a cat. Step 50: Noise has piled up so much you can barely tell it's a cat. Step 1000: The schedule has added so much noise that the image is now just random static, following the exact same plan every time.

cards.title: Myth: Noise Is Random Chaos Each Time
cards.body: Myth: 'The amount of noise added is different and unpredictable every time you run it.' Truth: The schedule itself is fixed and predetermined — the same amount of noise is added at each timestep every single time. Only the exact noise values are random; the *schedule* controlling how much to add is not.

cards.title: Fixed Plan vs. Random Guessing
cards.body: A noise schedule is like a planned staircase down into fog, not a random leap.

left.text: Predetermined steps, same amount of noise added at each timestep, every time

right.text: Unpredictable amount added, different every time, no consistent plan

exercises.prompt: What does a noise schedule control?

options.text: How much noise is added at each timestep
Which colors the final image will have
How the AI names the image file
How fast the computer's fan spins

exercises.explain: The noise schedule is the plan that decides exactly how much Gaussian noise gets mixed in at every single step of the process.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The noise schedule adds a totally different, unpredictable amount of noise every time the model runs.
exercises.explain: False! The schedule is fixed and predetermined — it follows the same plan for how much noise to add at each timestep every time.

exercises.sentence: At each timestep, a predetermined amount of ___ noise is mixed into the image following a fixed schedule.
exercises.explain: Gaussian noise is the type of random noise used — it follows a bell-curve pattern and is added according to the fixed schedule.

exercises.prompt: Why is it helpful for the noise schedule to be fixed and predetermined instead of random?

options.text: It makes the process consistent and predictable, so the model can learn how to reverse it step by step
It makes the image load faster on a phone screen
It stops the computer from ever making mistakes

exercises.explain: A fixed schedule means the model always knows exactly how much noise was added at each step, which makes it possible to learn a reliable way to reverse the process later.

exercises.prompt: Boss Challenge: A diffusion model is trained using a noise schedule where a set amount of Gaussian noise is added at each of 1000 timesteps. What best describes this process?

options.text: The image degrades gradually and predictably, following the same planned amount of noise at every step
The image is destroyed instantly in one single step with no plan
The amount of noise added changes randomly with no fixed plan each run
The noise schedule only applies to the very last timestep

exercises.explain: That's it! A noise schedule spreads the noise-adding process across many small, planned steps — like tea steeping or food coloring spreading — turning a clear image into static gradually and predictably.
